questions.question: Heat is a form of_______.
questions.question_raw: Heat is a form of_______.
questions.explanation: Heat is the amount of energy flowing from one body of matter to another spontaneously due to their temperature difference.

questions.question: Heat flows from one body to another if:
questions.question_raw: Heat flows from one body to another if:

options.label: A
options.text: there is a difference of material

options.label: B
options.text: there is a difference in temperatures

options.label: C
options.text: there is a difference of density

options.label: D
options.text: there is no difference in temperature

correct_option_texts: there is a difference in temperatures
questions.explanation: Heat is the amount of energy flowing from one body of matter to another spontaneously due to their temperature difference.

questions.question: The ice and steam point have values 32^oF and 212^oF respectively, on the Fahrenheit scale. Corresponding values on the Centigrade scale are:
questions.question_raw: The ice and steam point have values 32oF and 212oF respectively, on the Fahrenheit scale. Corresponding values on the Centigrade scale are:
questions.explanation:
${C \over 5} = {{F - 32} \over 9}$
 for ice , at 32 ^0F ; C= 0^ 0C
for steam , at 212^ 0F ; C= 5 $\times$ $212-32\over 9$= 100 ^0C

questions.question: 180 equal intervals between two reference points on the Fahrenheit scale correspond to:
questions.question_raw: 180 equal intervals between two reference points on the Fahrenheit scale correspond to:

options.label: A
options.text: 50 equal intervals on the Celsius scale

options.label: B
options.text: 110 equal intervals on the Celsius scale

options.label: C
options.text: 100 equal intervals on the Celsius scale

options.label: D
options.text: 90 equal intervals on the Celsius scale

correct_option_texts: 100 equal intervals on the Celsius scale
questions.explanation: ${{\Delta {T_C}} \over {100}} = {{\Delta {T_F}} \over {180}}$

questions.question: The correct equation for conversion between Fahrenheit temperature t_F and Celsius temperature t_C is:
questions.question_raw: The correct equation for conversion between Fahrenheit temperature tF and Celsius temperature tC is:
questions.explanation:
$\frac{T_Z-T_L}{T_U-T_L}=\frac{T_C-0}{100-0}=\frac{T_F-32}{212-32}$ 
TU = B.P. of water
TL = M.P. of ice

questions.question: If P is the pressure, $\mu $ is the number of moles in the sample, T the temperature and V the volume of gas, the ideal-gas equation is:
questions.question_raw: If P is the pressure, {tex}\mu {/tex} is the number of moles in the sample, T the temperature and V the volume of gas, the ideal-gas equation is:
questions.explanation:
$V \propto \frac{NT}{P}$
$\frac{PV}{NT} = k$
$N = \mu {N_A}$
$\frac{PV}{{\mu T}} = {N_A}k = R$
PV = $ \mu RT$

questions.question: Plots of pressure as a function of temperature for gas thermometers containing different types and quantities of gas converge to the same point when extrapolated to zero pressure. The temperature value to which converge is.
questions.question_raw: Plots of pressure as a function of temperature for gas thermometers containing different types and quantities of gas converge to the same point when extrapolated to zero pressure. The temperature value to which converge is.
questions.explanation: Absolute zero temp. = 0K

questions.question: Relation between Kelvin (T) and Celsius scales (t_C) is

questions.question: If the substance is in the form of a long rod, then for small change in temperature, $\Delta {\rm{T}}$, the fractional change in length, $\frac {\Delta l} {l}$, is
questions.question_raw: If the substance is in the form of a long rod, then for small change in temperature, {tex}\Delta {\rm{T}}{/tex}, the fractional change in length, {tex}\frac {\Delta l} {l}{/tex}, is
questions.explanation:
$\alpha=\frac{\Delta l}{l \times \Delta T}$
$\frac{\Delta l}{l}=a \Delta T$
where $\alpha$ is the temperature coefficient of linear expansion

questions.question: compared to glass copper expands:
questions.question_raw: compared to glass copper expands:
questions.explanation: The coefficient of linear expansion is more for copper than that of glass.

questions.question: If V is the initial volume, $\Delta {\rm{V}}$ the fractional change in volume of a substance for temperature change $\Delta {\rm{T}}$, the coefficient of volume expansion, ${\gamma _{\rm{V}}}$ is defined as:
questions.question_raw: If V is the initial volume, {tex}\Delta {\rm{V}}{/tex} the fractional change in volume of a substance for temperature change {tex}\Delta {\rm{T}}{/tex}, the coefficient of volume expansion, {tex}{\gamma _{\rm{V}}}{/tex} is defined as:
questions.explanation: Increase in volume per unit original volume per Kelvin rise in temperature is called coefficient of volume expansion, $\gamma_V=\left(\frac{\Delta V}{V}\right) \frac{1}{\Delta T}$

questions.question: Which of these materials has a low coefficient of volume expansion?
questions.question_raw: Which of these materials has a low coefficient of volume expansion?
questions.explanation: Glass is a ceramic material made from silica. So that it has a lower coefficient of volume expansion in comparison to metal and liquid.

questions.question: Which of these materials have high coefficients of volume expansion ${\alpha _{\rm{V}}}$?
questions.question_raw: Which of these materials have high coefficients of volume expansion {tex}{\alpha _{\rm{V}}}{/tex}?
questions.explanation: Alcohol has a higher coefficient of volume expansion in comparison to glass and Invar.

questions.question: expansion of water is called anomalous because:
questions.question_raw: expansion of water is called anomalous because:

options.label: A
options.text: it contracts on heating from 0^oC to 4^oC

options.label: B
options.text: it expands on heating from 0^oC to 4^oC

options.label: C
options.text: it contracts on cooling from 4^oC to 0^oC

options.label: D
options.text: it expands on cooling from 10^oC to 4^oC

correct_option_texts: it contracts on heating from 0^oC to 4^oC
questions.explanation: Liquids expand on heating and contract on cooling at moderate temperatures. But water exhibits exceptional behaviour. When it is heated from 0^oC, it actually contracts rather than expanding, up to 4^oC. The volume of water is minimum at 4^oC. Beyond 4^oC, water starts expanding. This behavior of water between 0^oC and 4^oC is called the anomalous expansion of water.

questions.question: Animal and plant life in lakes and ponds are not destroyed because:
questions.question_raw: Animal and plant life in lakes and ponds are not destroyed because:

options.label: A
options.text: water expands on heating at all temperatures

options.label: B
options.text: water contracts on cooling at all temperatures

options.label: C
options.text: freezing takes place at the top first and bottom layers are not frozen as temperature in bottom layers is more than zero

options.label: D
options.text: freezing takes place at the bottom first

correct_option_texts: freezing takes place at the top first and bottom layers are not frozen as temperature in bottom layers is more than zero
questions.explanation: As and when water is cooled down to its freezing point the thickness T of the ice layer increases. The increase in thickness of ice decreases the rate of cooling of water and thus freezing process slows down.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Thermal Properties of Matter

questions.question: For an ideal gas the coefficient of volume expansion at constant pressure is:
questions.question_raw: For an ideal gas the coefficient of volume expansion at constant pressure is:
questions.explanation:
$\gamma=\frac{\Delta V}{V \Delta T}$
PV = nRT
V = $\frac{n R T}{P}$
$P \Delta V=n R \Delta T$
$\Delta V=\frac{n R \Delta T}{P}$
$\gamma=\frac{\frac{n R \Delta T}{P}}{\frac{n R T}{P}} \times \frac{1}{\Delta T}$ = $\frac{n R \Delta T}{P} \times \frac{P}{n R T} \times \frac{1}{\Delta T}=\frac{1}{T}$

questions.question: The coefficient of volume expansion is n times coefficient of linear expansion where n equals:
questions.question_raw: The coefficient of volume expansion is n times coefficient of linear expansion where n equals:
questions.explanation:
$\alpha = \left( {{{\Delta l} \over l}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}}$ and $\gamma = \left( {{{\Delta V} \over V}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}}$ 
Volume V = L^3
expanded length = $L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ 
expanded Volume = ${\left[ {L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)} \right]^3} = {L^3}(1 + {\alpha ^2}\Delta {T^2} + 2\alpha \Delta T)(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ 
very small term is neglected
expanded volume = $V(1 + 3\alpha \Delta T)$ 
$\frac{\Delta l}{l}=a \Delta T$
$\gamma = \left( {{{\Delta V} \over V}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}} = {{3\alpha V\Delta T} \over V}{1 \over {\Delta T}}$ 
$ \gamma = 3\alpha$

questions.question: The coefficient of area expansion is n times of coefficient of linear expansion. where n equals
questions.question_raw: The coefficient of area expansion is n times of coefficient of linear expansion. where n equals
questions.explanation:
$\alpha = \left( {{{\Delta l} \over l}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}}$ and $\beta = \left( {{{\Delta A} \over A}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}}$ 
area A = L^2
expanded length = $L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ 
expanded area = ${\left[ {L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)} \right]^2} $ = $ {L^2}(1 + {\alpha ^2}\Delta {T^2} + 2\alpha \Delta T)$ 
${\alpha ^2}\Delta {T^2}$ is very small
expanded area = $A(1 + 2\alpha \Delta T)$ 
$\Delta A = 2\alpha A\Delta T$
$\beta=\left(\frac{\Delta A}{A}\right) \frac{1}{\Delta T}=\frac{2 \alpha A \Delta T}{A} \frac{1}{\Delta T}$
$\beta=2 \alpha$ 
So that n = 2

questions.question: Thermal stress is stress-induced in solids:
questions.question_raw: Thermal stress is stress-induced in solids:

options.label: A
options.text: if thermal expansion is allowed by floating it’s ends

options.label: C
options.text: due to dropping in cold water

options.label: D
options.text: if thermal expansion is prevented by fixing its ends rigidly

correct_option_texts: if thermal expansion is prevented by fixing its ends rigidly
questions.explanation: If temperature deformation is permitted to occur freely, no load or stress will be induced in the structure. In some cases where temperature deformation is not permitted, internal stress is created. The internal stress created is termed as thermal stress.

questions.question: If m is the mass of a substance, $\Delta {\rm{Q}}$ the energy required to raise the temperature by $\Delta {\rm{T}}$ the specific heat is given by:
questions.question_raw: If m is the mass of a substance, {tex}\Delta {\rm{Q}}{/tex} the energy required to raise the temperature by {tex}\Delta {\rm{T}}{/tex} the specific heat is given by:
questions.explanation: The specific heat is the amount of heat per unit mass required to raise the temperature by one degree Celsius.

questions.question: The latent heat of fusion is. (L_f)
questions.question_raw: The latent heat of fusion is. (Lf)

options.label: A
options.text: the heat per unit mass required for changing a substance from liquid into solid, at the same temperature and pressure

options.label: B
options.text: the heat per unit mass required for changing a substance from solid into liquid, at high pressure and low temperature

options.label: C
options.text: the heat per unit mass required for changing a substance from solid into liquid, at high temperature and low pressure

options.label: D
options.text: the heat per unit mass required for changing a substance from solid into liquid, at the same temperature and pressure

correct_option_texts: the heat per unit mass required for changing a substance from solid into liquid, at the same temperature and pressure
questions.explanation: The enthalpy of fusion of a substance, also known as (latent) heat of fusion, is the change in its enthalpy resulting from providing energy, typically heat, to a specific quantity of the substance to change its state from a solid to a liquid at constant pressure.

questions.question: The latent heat of vaporization $\left( {{{\rm{L}}_{\rm{v}}}} \right)$ is:
questions.question_raw: The latent heat of vaporization {tex}\left( {{{\rm{L}}_{\rm{v}}}} \right){/tex} is:

options.label: A
options.text: the heat per unit mass required to change a substance from liquid to the vapor state at high pressure and low temperature

options.label: B
options.text: the heat per unit mass required to change a substance from liquid to the vapor state without a change in the temperature and pressure.

options.label: C
options.text: the heat per unit mass required to change a substance from vapor to the liquid state without a change in the temperature and pressure.

options.label: D
options.text: the heat per unit mass required to change a substance from liquid to the vapor state, at high temperature and low pressure

correct_option_texts: the heat per unit mass required to change a substance from liquid to the vapor state without a change in the temperature and pressure.
questions.explanation: The latent heat of vaporization is actually the total amount of enthalpy (a kind of energy/heat) necessary to accomplish a phase change for a liquid or gas at the boiling/condensation point. Phase changes are generally considered at constant pressure, rather than constant volume.

questions.question: In which mode of heat transfer no medium is required?
questions.question_raw: In which mode of heat transfer no medium is required?
questions.explanation: Heat transfer through radiation takes place in the form of electromagnetic waves.

questions.question: In which mode of heat transfer is there material movement?
questions.question_raw: In which mode of heat transfer is there material movement?
questions.explanation: Convection is the heat transfer due to the bulk movement of molecules within fluids.

questions.question: If k is the thermal conductivity of the material of the bar of cross-section A whose ends are maintained at temperatures T_C and T_D, the rate of flow of heat H is:
questions.question_raw: If k is the thermal conductivity of the material of the bar of cross-section A whose ends are maintained at temperatures TC and TD, the rate of flow of heat H is:
questions.explanation:
rate of flow of heat H depends on
H $\propto$ A
H $\propto$ $\Delta$T
$H \propto \frac{1}{L}$ 
hence $H \propto \frac{A\Delta T}{L}$ 
$H = K\frac{A\Delta T}{L}$ 
K = thermal conductivity of the material

questions.question: If T_1 is the temperature of the surrounding medium and T_2 is the temperature of the body, then according to Newton’s Law of Cooling, rate of cooling of the body is:
questions.question_raw: If T1 is the temperature of the surrounding medium and T2 is the temperature of the body, then according to Newton’s Law of Cooling, rate of cooling of the body is:
questions.explanation: Newton's law of cooling states that the rate of heat loss of a body is directly proportional to the difference in the temperatures between the body and its surroundings.

questions.question: The average kinetic energy of the molecules in a substance is most closely associated with:
questions.question_raw: The average kinetic energy of the molecules in a substance is most closely associated with:
questions.explanation: $E = {3 \over 2}kT$

questions.question: Which of the following is true of the Celsius and Kelvin temperature scales?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is true of the Celsius and Kelvin temperature scales?

options.label: A
options.text: Both the Celsius and Kelvin temperature scales have negative values.

options.label: B
options.text: A Kelvin degree is smaller in size than a Celsius degree.

options.label: C
options.text: A Kelvin degree is larger in size than a Celsius degree.

options.label: D
options.text: A Kelvin degree and a Celsius degree are equivalent in size.

correct_option_texts: A Kelvin degree and a Celsius degree are equivalent in size.

questions.question: In general, when a solid is heated, it:

options.text: contracts proportionally to the change in temperature

questions.explanation:
$\Delta l=\beta V \Delta T $
$\Delta A=\alpha V \Delta T$
$\Delta V=\gamma V \Delta T$

questions.question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?
 Every substance contains heat.
 For heat to flow between two substances, they must be at different temperatures.
 The internal energy of a substance is equal to the kinetic energy of the molecules in the substance.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

  1. Every substance contains heat.
  2. For heat to flow between two substances, they must be at different temperatures.
  3. The internal energy of a substance is equal to the kinetic energy of the molecules in the substance.
questions.explanation: Heat is the form of energy that is transferred from one body to another as the result of a difference in temperature.

questions.question: A washer consists of a 3.00 cm diameter circle of sheet metal with a 1.00 cm diameter circular hole in the middle. If the metal washer is heated until the diameter of the washer is 3.03 cm, then the diameter of the hole will be:
questions.question_raw: A washer consists of a 3.00 cm diameter circle of sheet metal with a 1.00 cm diameter circular hole in the middle. If the metal washer is heated until the diameter of the washer is 3.03 cm, then the diameter of the hole will be:
questions.explanation:
$\alpha=\frac{\Delta l}{l \Delta T}$
$\frac{0.03}{3 \Delta T}=\frac{\Delta l}{1 \Delta T}$
$\Delta l$ = 0.01 cm
diameter of hole = 1 + 0.01 = 1.01 cm

questions.question: Find the wrong statement about the specific heat of a material:
questions.question_raw: Find the wrong statement about the specific heat of a material:
questions.explanation: extensive properties (i.e. mass) are dependent upon the amount of a substance, while intensive properties (i.e. density) are independent of quantity.

questions.question: Molar Specific Heat capacity has units of:
questions.question_raw: Molar Specific Heat capacity has units of:
questions.explanation:
Q = nC$\Delta $T
C = $Q\over{n\Delta T}$

questions.question: Thermal conductivity has units of:
questions.question_raw: Thermal conductivity has units of:
questions.explanation:
$H=\frac{K A \Delta \theta}{l}$
$K=\frac{H l}{A \Delta \theta}$
k- coefficient of thermal conductivity,
H - heat flowing per unit time,
l - length of solid material,
A- area of cross section, $\Delta \theta$ - temperature difference

questions.question: Two absolute scales A and B have triple points of water defined to be 200 A and 350 B. What is the relation between T_A and T_B?
questions.question_raw: Two absolute scales A and B have triple points of water defined to be 200 A and 350 B. What is the relation between TA and TB?
questions.explanation:
for any temperature scale 
$\frac{X}{X_{T r}}$ = const.
$\frac{T_{A}}{200}=\frac{T_{B}}{350}$
$T_{A}=\frac{4}{7} T_{B}$

questions.question: The electrical resistance in ohms of a certain thermometer varies with temperature according to the approximate law: R = R$_{\rm{o}}$ [1 + $\alpha $ (T – T$_{\rm{o}}$ )] The resistance is 101.6 $\Omega $ at the triple-point of water 273.16 K, and 165.5 $\Omega $ at the normal melting point of lead (600.5 K). What is the temperature when the resistance is 123.4 $\Omega $?
questions.question_raw: The electrical resistance in ohms of a certain thermometer varies with temperature according to the approximate law: R = R{tex}_{\rm{o}}{/tex} [1 + {tex}\alpha {/tex} (T – T{tex}_{\rm{o}}{/tex} )] The resistance is 101.6 {tex}\Omega {/tex} at the triple-point of water 273.16 K, and 165.5 {tex}\Omega {/tex} at the normal melting point of lead (600.5 K). What is the temperature when the resistance is 123.4 {tex}\Omega {/tex}?
questions.explanation:
It is given that:
R = R_0 [1 + $\alpha $ (T - T_0)] …(i)
where,
R_0 and T_0 are the initial resistance and temperature respectively
R and T are the final resistance and temperature respectively 
$\alpha $ is a constant
At the triple point of water, T_0 = 273.15 K
Resistance of lead, R_0 = 101.6 $\Omega $
At normal melting point of lead, T = 600.5 K
The resistance of lead, R = 165.5 $\Omega $
Substituting these values in equation (i), we get:
R = R_o [1 + $\alpha $ (T - T_o)]
165.5 = 101.6 [ 1 + $\alpha $(600.5 - 273.15) ] 
1.629 = 1 + $\alpha $(327.35) 
∴ $\alpha $ = $0.629 \over 327.35$ = 1.92 $\times$ 10^-3 K^-1
For resistance, R_1 = 123.4 $\Omega $
R_1 = R_0 [1 + $\alpha$ (T - T_0)]
where,
T is the temperature when the resistance of lead is 123.4 $\Omega $
123.4 = 101.6 [ 1 + 1.92 $\times$ 10^-3( T - 273.15) ]
Solving for T, we get
T = 384.8 K.

questions.question: A steel tape 1m long is correctly calibrated for a temperature of 27.0^oC. The length of a steel rod measured by this tape is found to be 63.0 cm on a hot day when the temperature is 45.0 ^oC. What is the actual length of the steel rod on that day? Coefficient of linear expansion of steel = 1.20 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1.
questions.question_raw: A steel tape 1m long is correctly calibrated for a temperature of 27.0oC. The length of a steel rod measured by this tape is found to be 63.0 cm on a hot day when the temperature is 45.0 oC. What is the actual length of the steel rod on that day? Coefficient of linear expansion of steel = 1.20 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1.
questions.explanation:
$\Delta l = \alpha l\Delta T $ = 1.20 $\times$ 10^−5 $\times$ 63 $\times$ 18 = 0.0136cm
final length = $l +\Delta l$ = 63.0136cm

questions.question: A large steel wheel is to be fitted on to a shaft of the same material. At 27 $^\circ $C, the outer diameter of the shaft is 8.70 cm and the diameter of the central hole in the wheel is 8.69 cm. The shaft is cooled using ‘dry ice’. At what temperature of the shaft does the wheel slip on the shaft? Assume the coefficient of linear expansion of the steel to be constant over the required temperature range: ${\alpha _{{\rm{steel}}}}$ = 1.20 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1.
questions.question_raw: A large steel wheel is to be fitted on to a shaft of the same material. At 27 {tex}^\circ {/tex}C, the outer diameter of the shaft is 8.70 cm and the diameter of the central hole in the wheel is 8.69 cm. The shaft is cooled using ‘dry ice’. At what temperature of the shaft does the wheel slip on the shaft? Assume the coefficient of linear expansion of the steel to be constant over the required temperature range: {tex}{\alpha _{{\rm{steel}}}}{/tex} = 1.20 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1.
questions.explanation:
$\alpha=\frac{\Delta l}{l \Delta T}$
$\Delta T=\frac{\Delta l}{l \alpha}=\frac{0.01}{8.70 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5}}=96 C^{\circ}$
final temp. = 27 − 96 = −69^oC

questions.question: A hole is drilled in a copper sheet. The diameter of the hole is 4.24 cm at 27.0^oC. What is the change in the diameter of the hole when the sheet is heated to 227^oC? Coefficient of linear expansion of copper = 1.70 $ \times $ 10^-5 K^–1.
questions.question_raw: A hole is drilled in a copper sheet. The diameter of the hole is 4.24 cm at 27.0oC. What is the change in the diameter of the hole when the sheet is heated to 227oC? Coefficient of linear expansion of copper = 1.70 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-5 K–1.
questions.explanation:
Change in diameter
$\Delta l = \alpha l\Delta T $
= 1.70 $\times$ 10^−5 $\times$ 4.24 $\times$ (227 − 27) = 1.44 $\times$10^−2 cm

questions.question: A brass wire 1.8 m long at 27 ^oC is held taut with little tension between two rigid supports. If the wire is cooled to a temperature of –39 $^\circ $C, what is the tension developed in the wire, if its diameter is 2.0 mm? Co-efficient of linear expansion of brass = 2.0 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1; Young’s modulus of brass = 0.91 $ \times $ 10^11 Pa.
questions.question_raw: A brass wire 1.8 m long at 27 oC is held taut with little tension between two rigid supports. If the wire is cooled to a temperature of –39 {tex}^\circ {/tex}C, what is the tension developed in the wire, if its diameter is 2.0 mm? Co-efficient of linear expansion of brass = 2.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1; Young’s modulus of brass = 0.91 {tex} \times {/tex} 1011 Pa.
questions.explanation:
Initial temperature, T_1 = 27^oC
Length of the brass wire at T_1, l = 1.8 m
Final temperature, T_2 = –39^oC
Diameter of the wire, d = 2.0 mm = 2 $\times$ 10^–3 m
Tension developed in the wire = F
Coefficient of linear expansion of brass, α = 2.0 $\times$ 10^–5 K^–1
Young’s modulus of brass, Y = 0.91 $\times$ 10^11 Pa
Young’s modulus is given by the relation:
γ = $Stress \over Strain$ = ${{F\over A} \over {\Delta L\over L}}$
$\Delta L = {{FL} \over {YA}}$ ....
Where,
F = Tension developed in the wire 
A = Area of a cross-section of the wire. 
$\Delta L$ = Change in the length, given by the relation:
$\Delta L = \alpha L({T_2} - {T_1})$ … (ii)
Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get:
$\alpha L\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)=\frac{F L}{Y A}$
$\alpha L\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)=\frac{F L}{Y \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}}$
$F=\alpha\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right) Y \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}$
F = 2 $\times$ 10^-5 $\times$ (-39 - 27) $\times$ 3.14 $\times$ 0.91 $\times$ 10^11 $\times$ ($2 \times 10^{-3} \over 2$ )^2
= -3.8 $\times$ 10^2 N
(The negative sign indicates that the tension is directed inward.)
Hence, the tension developed in the wire is 3.8 $\times$10^2 N.

questions.question: A brass rod of length 50 cm and diameter 3.0 mm is joined to a steel rod of the same length and diameter. What is the change in length of the combined rod at 250 $^\circ $C, if the original lengths are at 40.0 $^\circ $C? The ends of the rod are free to expand (Co-efficient of linear expansion of brass = 2.0 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1, steel = 1.2 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1).
questions.question_raw: A brass rod of length 50 cm and diameter 3.0 mm is joined to a steel rod of the same length and diameter. What is the change in length of the combined rod at 250 {tex}^\circ {/tex}C, if the original lengths are at 40.0 {tex}^\circ {/tex}C? The ends of the rod are free to expand (Co-efficient of linear expansion of brass = 2.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1, steel = 1.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1).
questions.explanation:
For the expansion in the brass rod
$\frac{\Delta l_{1}}{l_{1}}=\alpha_{1} \Delta T$
$\Delta l_{1}=l_{1} \alpha_{1} \Delta T=50 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5} \times 210$ = 0.2205 cm
For the expansion in the steel rod
$\frac{\Delta l_{2}}{l_{2}}=\alpha_{2} \Delta T$
$\Delta l_{2}=l_{2} \alpha_{2} \Delta T=50 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 210=0.126 \mathrm{cm}$ 
Total change in the lengths of brass and steel
$\Delta l = \Delta {l_1} + \Delta {l_2} $ = 0.2205 + 0.126 = 0.346cm
Total change in the length of the combined rod = 0.346 cm

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Thermal Properties of Matter

questions.question: The coefficient of volume expansion of glycerin is 49 $ \times $ 10^−5 /^ 0C. What is the fractional change in its density for a 30 ^oC rise in temperature?
questions.question_raw: The coefficient of volume expansion of glycerin is 49 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 / 0C. What is the fractional change in its density for a 30 oC rise in temperature?
questions.explanation:
Coefficient of volume expansion of glycerine,
γ=49×10^-5/ ^0C
Rise in temperature, ΔT=30^ 0C
As, $\gamma = {1\over \Delta T}$$({\Delta V\over V})$
$({\Delta V\over V})$= $\gamma\times \Delta T$
or ΔV/V=(49×10^-5)×30=0.0147
Since mass remains constant,
Fractional change in density = fractional change in volume = 0.0147

questions.question: A 10 kW drilling machine is used to drill a bore in a small aluminum block of mass 8.0 kg. How much is the rise in temperature of the block in 2.5 minutes, assuming 50 percent of power is used up in heating the machine itself or lost to the surroundings. Specific heat of aluminum = 0.91 J g^−1K^−1.
questions.question_raw: A 10 kW drilling machine is used to drill a bore in a small aluminum block of mass 8.0 kg. How much is the rise in temperature of the block in 2.5 minutes, assuming 50 percent of power is used up in heating the machine itself or lost to the surroundings. Specific heat of aluminum = 0.91 J g−1K−1.
questions.explanation:
W = Pt = 10 $\times$ 10^3 $\times$ 2.5 $\times$ 60 = 150 $\times$ 10^4J
Energy used in heating = 50% = 75 $\times$ 10^4J
Q = ms$\Delta$T
$ \Delta T = {Q \over {ms}} $
$\Delta T = {{75 \times {{10}^4}} \over {8000 \times 0.91}}$
$\Delta T $ = 103^oC

questions.question: A copper block of mass 2.5 kg is heated in a furnace to a temperature of 500 ^oC and then placed on a large ice block. What is the maximum amount of ice that can melt? (Specific heat of copper = 0.39 J g^−1K^−1; the heat of fusion of water = 335 J g^−1).
questions.question_raw: A copper block of mass 2.5 kg is heated in a furnace to a temperature of 500 oC and then placed on a large ice block. What is the maximum amount of ice that can melt? (Specific heat of copper = 0.39 J g−1K−1; the heat of fusion of water = 335 J g−1).
questions.explanation:
Heat given by Cu = Heat Intake by ice
mC_us$\Delta$T = m_iceL
2500 $\times$ 0.39 $\times$ 500 = m_ice $\times$ 335
m_ice = 1.5Kg

questions.question: In an experiment on the specific heat of a metal, a 0.2 kg block of the metal at 150 ^oC is dropped in a copper calorimeter (of water equivalent 0.025 kg) containing 150 cm^3 of water at 27 ^oC. The final temperature is 40 ^oC, the specific heat of the metal will be
questions.question_raw: In an experiment on the specific heat of a metal, a 0.2 kg block of the metal at 150 oC is dropped in a copper calorimeter (of water equivalent 0.025 kg) containing 150 cm3 of water at 27 oC. The final temperature is 40 oC, the specific heat of the metal will be
questions.explanation:
Heat lost by the metal = Heat gained by the water and calorimeter system
$m_{1} c_{1} \Delta T_{1}=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) c_{2} \Delta T_{2}$
200 $\times$ c_1 $\times$ 110 = (150 + 25) $\times$ 4.186 $\times$ 13
$c_{1}=\frac{175 \times 4.186 \times 13}{200 \times 110}$
c_1 = 0.43Jg^−1K^−1

questions.question: A child running a temperature of 101^oF is given an antipyrin (i.e. medicine that lowers fever) which causes an increase in the rate of evaporation of sweat from his body. If the fever is brought down to 98 ^oF in 20 min, what is the average rate of extra evaporation caused, by the drug? Assume the evaporation mechanism to be the only way by which heat is lost. The mass of the child is 30 kg. The specific heat of human body is approximately the same as that of water, and the latent heat of evaporation of water at that temperature is about 580 cal g^−1.
questions.question_raw: A child running a temperature of 101oF is given an antipyrin (i.e. medicine that lowers fever) which causes an increase in the rate of evaporation of sweat from his body. If the fever is brought down to 98 oF in 20 min, what is the average rate of extra evaporation caused, by the drug? Assume the evaporation mechanism to be the only way by which heat is lost. The mass of the child is 30 kg. The specific heat of human body is approximately the same as that of water, and the latent heat of evaporation of water at that temperature is about 580 cal g−1.
questions.explanation:
$\Delta T = 101 - 98 = 3^\circ F = \left( {3 \times {5 \over 9}} \right)^\circ C$ 
Specific heat of the human body = Specific heat of water = 1000 cal/kg/ ^oC
The heat lost by the child is Q = $ mc\Delta T = 30 \times 1000 \times \left( {3 \times {5 \over 9}} \right) $ = 50000cal
Let m_1 be the mass of the water evaporated from the child’s body in 20 min. 
Loss of heat through water is
Q = m_1L
${m_1} = {Q \over L} = {{50000} \over {580}} $ = 86.2gm
The average rate of extra evaporation caused by the drug = $m_1 \over t$
= $86.2 \over 20$
= 4.3 g/min.

questions.question: A ‘thermal’ icebox is a cheap and efficient method for storing small quantities of cooked food in summer in particular. A cubical icebox of side 30 cm has a thickness of 5.0 cm. If 4.0 kg of ice is put in the box, estimate the amount of ice remaining after 6 h. The outside temperature is 45 ^oC, and the coefficient of thermal conductivity of thermal is 0.01 J s^−1m^−1K^−1. [Heat of fusion of water = 335 $ \times $ 10^3Jkg^−1]
questions.question_raw: A ‘thermal’ icebox is a cheap and efficient method for storing small quantities of cooked food in summer in particular. A cubical icebox of side 30 cm has a thickness of 5.0 cm. If 4.0 kg of ice is put in the box, estimate the amount of ice remaining after 6 h. The outside temperature is 45 oC, and the coefficient of thermal conductivity of thermal is 0.01 J s−1m−1K−1. [Heat of fusion of water = 335 {tex} \times {/tex} 103Jkg−1]
questions.explanation:
$Q = {{KA\Delta T} \over l}t$ 
A = Surface area of the box = 6s^2 = 6 $\times$ (0.3)^2 = 0.54 m^3
Time gap, t = 6 h = 6 $\times$ 60 $\times$ 60 s
$Q = {{0.01 \times 0.54 \times 45 \times 6 \times 60 \times 60} \over {0.05}} $ = 104976J
But Q= m'L
$\therefore$ m' = $Q\over L$
= $104976\over(335 \times 10^3)$ = 0.313 kg
Mass of ice left = 4 – 0.3 = 3.7 kg
Hence, the amount of ice remaining after 6 h is 3.7 kg.

questions.question: A brass boiler has a base area of 0.15 m^2 and thickness 1.0 cm. It boils water at a rate of 6.0 kg/min when placed on a gas stove. Estimate the temperature of the part of the flame in contact with the boiler. Thermal conductivity of brass = 109 J s^−1m^−1K^−1 ; Heat of vaporization of water = 2256 $ \times $ 10^3Jkg^−1.
questions.question_raw: A brass boiler has a base area of 0.15 m2 and thickness 1.0 cm. It boils water at a rate of 6.0 kg/min when placed on a gas stove. Estimate the temperature of the part of the flame in contact with the boiler. Thermal conductivity of brass = 109 J s−1m−1K−1 ; Heat of vaporization of water = 2256 {tex} \times {/tex} 103Jkg−1.
questions.explanation:
The base area of the boiler, A = 0.15 m^2
Thickness of the boiler, l = 1.0 cm = 0.01 m
Boiling rate of water, R = 6.0 kg/min
Mass, m = 6 kg
Time, t = 1 min = 60 s
Thermal conductivity of brass, K = 109 J s ^–1 m^–1 K^–1
Heat of vaporisation, L = 2256 × 10^3 J kg^–1
The amount of heat flowing into the water through the brass base of the boiler is
$Q = {{KA({T_1} - {T_2})} \over l}t$ ......(1)
where,
T_1 = Temperature of the flame in contact with the boiler
T_2 = Boiling point of water = 100°C 
Heat required for boiling the water
Q = mL....…(ii)
Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get:
mL = $KA(T_1 - T_2) t \over l $
T_1 - T_2 = $mLl \over KAt$
= $6 \times 2256 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \over(109 \times 0.15 \times 60)$
= 137.98 ^oC
Therefore, the temperature of the part of the flame in contact with the boiler is 237.98^oC.

questions.question: A body cools from 80 ^oC to 50 ^oC in 5 minutes. Calculate the time it takes to cool from 60 ^oC to 30 ^oC. The temperature of the surroundings is 20 ^oC.
questions.question_raw: A body cools from 80 oC to 50 oC in 5 minutes. Calculate the time it takes to cool from 60 oC to 30 oC. The temperature of the surroundings is 20 oC.
questions.explanation:
Initial temperature (T_i​) = 80 ^oC
Final temperature (T_f) = 50 ^oC
Temperature of the surrounding (T_0) = 20 ^oC
t = 5 min
According to Newton's law of cooling,
Rate of cooling $\frac {dt}{dT}$​ = $K({(T_i+T_F)\over 2}-T_0)$
$\frac {(T_f - T_i)}{t} = \frac {K[(80 + 50)]}{2-20}$
$\frac {80-50}{5}$​ = K[65 − 20]
6 = K $\times$ 45
K = ${6\over 45}={2\over 15}$
In second condition,
initial temperature =T_i = 60 ^oC
Final temperature T_f​ = 30 ^oC
Time taken for cooling is t
According to Newton's law of cooling 
$\frac {(60-30)}{t}$t ​ =${2\over 15}[{(60+30)\over 2}-20]$
$\frac {30}{t}=(\frac {2}{15})(25)$
$\therefore$ t = 9 min

questions.question: On a day when the temperature reaches 50^oF, what is the temperature in degrees Celsius and in kelvins?
questions.question_raw: On a day when the temperature reaches 50oF, what is the temperature in degrees Celsius and in kelvins?

questions.question: A steel railroad track has a length of 30.000 m when the temperature is 0.0^oC. What is its length when the temperature is 40.0$^\circ $ C? $\alpha$ = 11 $\times$ 10^−6 (^oC)^-1.
questions.question_raw: A steel railroad track has a length of 30.000 m when the temperature is 0.0oC. What is its length when the temperature is 40.0{tex}^\circ {/tex} C? {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−6 (oC)-1.
questions.explanation:
$\Delta l = \alpha l\Delta \theta $ =11 $\times$ 10^−6 $\times$ 30 $\times$ 40 = 0.013m
Total length after expansion = 30 + 0.013 = 30.013m

questions.question: A steel railroad track has a length of 30.000 m when the temperature is 0.0^oC. Suppose that the ends of the rail are rigidly clamped at 0.0^oC so that expansion is prevented. What is the thermal stress set up in the rail if its temperature is raised to 40.0^oC? $\alpha $− steel = 11 $\times$ 10^−6 (^oC)^-1 Y-steel = 20GPa?
questions.question_raw: A steel railroad track has a length of 30.000 m when the temperature is 0.0oC. Suppose that the ends of the rail are rigidly clamped at 0.0oC so that expansion is prevented. What is the thermal stress set up in the rail if its temperature is raised to 40.0oC? {tex}\alpha {/tex}− steel = 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−6 (oC)-1 Y-steel = 20GPa?
questions.explanation: Thermal Stress $ = Y\alpha \Delta \theta $ = 20 $\times$ 10^9 $\times$ 11 $\times$ 10^−6 $\times$ 40 = 8.8 $\times$ 10^6

questions.question: An ideal gas occupies a volume of 100 ${\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ at ${\rm{2}}0^\circ $ C and 100 Pa. Find the number of moles of gas in the container.
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas occupies a volume of 100 {tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{/tex} at {tex}{\rm{2}}0^\circ {/tex} C and 100 Pa. Find the number of moles of gas in the container.
questions.explanation:
number of moles according to gas equation
$n = {{PV} \over {RT}} = {{100 \times 100 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \over {8.31 \times 293}} = 4.10 \times {10^{ - 6}}$

questions.question: A spray can contain a propellant gas at twice atmospheric pressure (202 kPa) and having a volume of 125 cm^3 is at 22^oC. It is then tossed into an open fire. When the temperature of the gas in the can reaches 195^oC, what is the pressure inside the can? Assume any change in the volume of the can is negligible.
questions.question_raw: A spray can contain a propellant gas at twice atmospheric pressure (202 kPa) and having a volume of 125 cm3 is at 22oC. It is then tossed into an open fire. When the temperature of the gas in the can reaches 195oC, what is the pressure inside the can? Assume any change in the volume of the can is negligible.

questions.question: A student eats a dinner rated at 2000 Calories. He wishes to do an equivalent amount of work in the gymnasium by lifting a 50.0-kg barbell. How many times must he raise the barbell to expend this much energy? Assume that he raises the barbell 2.00 m each time he lifts it and that he regains no energy when he drops the barbell to the floor.
questions.question_raw: A student eats a dinner rated at 2000 Calories. He wishes to do an equivalent amount of work in the gymnasium by lifting a 50.0-kg barbell. How many times must he raise the barbell to expend this much energy? Assume that he raises the barbell 2.00 m each time he lifts it and that he regains no energy when he drops the barbell to the floor.
questions.explanation:
Total no. of times he lifts barbell = $Total \ energy \over energy \ in \ lifting \ barbell \ once$
$= {Q \over {mgh}}$
$= {{2000 \times 4.186 \times {{10}^3}} \over {50 \times 9.8 \times 2}} $ = 8.54 $\times$ 10^3 times

questions.question: A 0.0500-kg ingot of metal is heated to 200.0^oC and then dropped into a beaker containing 0.400 kg of water initially at 20.0^oC. If the final equilibrium temperature of the mixed system is 22.4^oC, find the specific heat of the metal.
questions.question_raw: A 0.0500-kg ingot of metal is heated to 200.0oC and then dropped into a beaker containing 0.400 kg of water initially at 20.0oC. If the final equilibrium temperature of the mixed system is 22.4oC, find the specific heat of the metal.

questions.question: A cowboy fires a silver bullet with a mass of 2.00 g and with a muzzle speed of 200 m/s into the pine wall of a saloon. Assume that all the internal energy generated by the impact remains with the bullet. What is the temperature change of the bullet? Specific heat of silver is 234J/Kg^oC?
questions.question_raw: A cowboy fires a silver bullet with a mass of 2.00 g and with a muzzle speed of 200 m/s into the pine wall of a saloon. Assume that all the internal energy generated by the impact remains with the bullet. What is the temperature change of the bullet? Specific heat of silver is 234J/KgoC?
questions.explanation:
Q = K
$mc\Delta T = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
$ \Delta T = {{{v^2}} \over {2c}} = {{200 \times 200} \over {2 \times 234}} = 85.5^\circ C $

questions.question:
What mass of steam initially at 130$^\circ $C is needed to warm 200 g of water in a 100-g glass container from 20.0$^\circ $C to 50.0^oC? Specific heat of container is 837J/Kg^oC 
Specific heat of steam is 2110J/Kg^oC 
Specific heat of water is 4186J/Kg^oC 
Latent heat of vapourisation is 2.26 $\times$ 10^6J/Kg
questions.question_raw:
What mass of steam initially at 130{tex}^\circ {/tex}C is needed to warm 200 g of water in a 100-g glass container from 20.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C to 50.0oC? Specific heat of container is 837J/KgoC  
Specific heat of steam is 2110J/KgoC 
Specific heat of water is 4186J/KgoC 
Latent heat of vapourisation is 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 106J/Kg
questions.explanation:
Heat given by steam = heat taken by water + heat taken by the container
$m{s_s}\Delta {T_1} + mL + m{s_w}\Delta {T_2} = m{s_w}\Delta {T_3} + m{s_c}\Delta {T_3}$
$\left( {m \times 2110 \times 30} \right) + \left( {m \times 2.26 \times {{10}^6}} \right) + \left( {m \times 4186 \times 50} \right) $$= \left( {0.2 \times 4186 \times 30} \right) + \left( {0.1 \times 837 \times 30} \right) $
after solving
mass of steam m = 10.9gm

questions.question: Liquid helium has a very low boiling point, 4.2 K, and a very low latent heat of vaporization, 2.09 $\times$ 10^4 J/kg. If energy is transferred to a container of boiling liquid helium from an immersed electric heater at a rate of 10.0 W, how long does it take to boil away 1.00 kg of the liquid?
questions.question_raw: Liquid helium has a very low boiling point, 4.2 K, and a very low latent heat of vaporization, 2.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 J/kg. If energy is transferred to a container of boiling liquid helium from an immersed electric heater at a rate of 10.0 W, how long does it take to boil away 1.00 kg of the liquid?
questions.explanation:
Q = mL= 1 $\times$ 2.09 $\times$ 10^4 = 2.09 $\times$10^4J
$\Delta t = {Q \over P} = {{2.09 \times {{10}^4}} \over {10}} $ = 2.09 $\times$ 10^3 sec = 35 min

questions.question: The temperature of a silver bar rises by 10.0$^\circ $C when it absorbs 1.23 kJ of energy by heat. The mass of the bar is 525 g. Determine the specific heat of silver?
questions.question_raw: The temperature of a silver bar rises by 10.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C when it absorbs 1.23 kJ of energy by heat. The mass of the bar is 525 g. Determine the specific heat of silver?
questions.explanation: $s = {Q \over {m\Delta T}} = {{1.23} \over {0.525 \times 10}} = 0.234KJ/Kg^\circ C$

questions.question: A 1.50-kg iron horseshoe initially at 600^oC is dropped into a bucket containing 20.0 kg of water at 25.0^oC. What is the final temperature? (Neglect the heat capacity of the container and assume that a negligible amount of water boils away.) Specific heat of iron = 448J/Kg^oC.
questions.question_raw: A 1.50-kg iron horseshoe initially at 600oC is dropped into a bucket containing 20.0 kg of water at 25.0oC. What is the final temperature? (Neglect the heat capacity of the container and assume that a negligible amount of water boils away.) Specific heat of iron = 448J/KgoC.
questions.explanation:
by the principle of calorimetry,
The heat has given by iron = Heat intake by water
$m_{F e} s_{F e} \Delta T_{1}=m_{w} s_{w} \Delta T_{2}$
1.5 $\times$ 448(600 - T) = 20 $\times$ 4186(T - 25)|
after solving
final temperature T = 29.6^oC

questions.question:
An aluminum calorimeter with a mass of 100 g contains 250 g of water. The calorimeter and water are in thermal equilibrium at 10.0$^\circ $C. Two metallic blocks are placed into the water. One is a 50.0-g piece of copper at 80.0$^\circ $C; the other block has a mass of 70.0 g and is originally at a temperature of 100$^\circ $C. The entire system stabilizes at a final temperature of 20.0$^\circ $C. Determine the specific heat of the unknown sample.
Specific heat of aluminium is 0.215cal/gm^oC 
Specific heat of copper is 0.0924cal/gm^oC
questions.question_raw:
An aluminum calorimeter with a mass of 100 g contains 250 g of water. The calorimeter and water are in thermal equilibrium at 10.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. Two metallic blocks are placed into the water. One is a 50.0-g piece of copper at 80.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C; the other block has a mass of 70.0 g and is originally at a temperature of 100{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. The entire system stabilizes at a final temperature of 20.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. Determine the specific heat of the unknown sample.
Specific heat of aluminium is  0.215cal/gmoC 
Specific heat of copper is 0.0924cal/gmoC
questions.explanation:
Heat given by Copper(1) + heat given by unknown metal(2) = heat gain by aluminum (3) + heat gain by water(4)
${m_1}{s_1}\Delta {T_1} + {m_2}{s_2}\Delta {T_2} $ = ${m_3}{s_3}\Delta {T_3} + {m_4}{s_4}\Delta {T_4} $ 
(50 $\times$ 0.0924 $\times$ 60) + (70 $\times$ s_2 $\times$ 80) = (100 $\times$ 0.215 $\times$10) + (250 $\times$ 1 $\times$10)
after solving 
specific heat of unknown metal s_2 = 0.435cal/gm^oC

questions.question: A 3.00-g copper penny at 25.0$^\circ $C drops from a height of 50.0 m to the ground. (a) If 60.0% of the change in potential energy goes into increasing the internal energy, what is its final temperature?
questions.question_raw: A 3.00-g copper penny at 25.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C drops from a height of 50.0 m to the ground. (a) If 60.0% of the change in potential energy goes into increasing the internal energy, what is its final temperature?
questions.explanation:
mgh = 3 $\times$ 10^−3$\times$10 $\times$ 50 = 1.5J
Heat used in increasing temp 
$Q=1.5 \times \frac{60}{100}=0.9 J$
Q = ms $\Delta$T_0.9 = 3 $\times$ 0.385 $\times$ (T−298)
T = 298.8K = 25.8^oC

questions.question: A water heater is operated by solar power. If the solar collector has an area of 6.00 m^2 and the power delivered by sunlight is 550 W/ m^2, how long does it take to increase the temperature of 1.00 m^3 of water from 20.0^oC to 60.0^oC?
questions.question_raw: A water heater is operated by solar power. If the solar collector has an area of 6.00 m2 and the power delivered by sunlight is 550 W/ m2, how long does it take to increase the temperature of 1.00 m3 of water from 20.0oC to 60.0oC?
questions.explanation:
$Q = ms\Delta T$
$ P = {W \over t} = {{ms\Delta T} \over t} $ 
$t = {{ms\Delta T} \over P} = {{1000 \times 4186 \times 40} \over {550 \times 6}} $ = 50700 sec = 50.7 ksec

questions.question: A 3.00-g lead bullet at 30.0^oC is fired at a speed of 240 m/s into a large block of ice at 0^oC, in which it becomes embedded. What quantity of ice melts? Latent heat of ice = 3.33 $\times$ 10^-5 J/Kg, the specific heat of lead = 128J/Kg^oC?
questions.question_raw: A 3.00-g lead bullet at 30.0oC is fired at a speed of 240 m/s into a large block of ice at 0oC, in which it becomes embedded. What quantity of ice melts? Latent heat of ice = 3.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 J/Kg, the specific heat of lead = 128J/KgoC?
questions.explanation:
${1 \over 2}{m_{Pb}}{v^2} + {m_{Pb}}{s_{Pb}}(30 - 0) = {m_{ice}}{L_{ice}}$
($1\over 2$ $\times$ 3 $\times$ 10^−3 $\times$ 240 $\times$ 240)+(3 $\times$ 10^−3 $\times$ 128 $\times$ 30) = m_ice $\times$ 3.33 $\times$ 10^5
m_ice = 2.94gm

questions.question: A 1.00-kg block of copper at 20.0$^\circ $C is dropped into a large vessel of liquid nitrogen at 77.3 K. How many kilograms of nitrogen boil away by the time the copper reaches 77.3 K? (The specific heat of copper is 0.092 0 cal/g. $^\circ $C. The latent heat of vaporization of nitrogen is 48.0 cal/g.)
questions.question_raw: A 1.00-kg block of copper at 20.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C is dropped into a large vessel of liquid nitrogen at 77.3 K. How many kilograms of nitrogen boil away by the time the copper reaches 77.3 K? (The specific heat of copper is 0.092 0 cal/g. {tex}^\circ {/tex}C. The latent heat of vaporization of nitrogen is 48.0 cal/g.)
questions.explanation:
Heat is given by copper = Heat gain by nitrogen
ms$\Delta$T = mN_2L
1000 $\times$ 0.092 $\times$ 215.7 = mN_2 × 48
mN_2 = 414gm = 0.414Kg

questions.question: In an insulated vessel, 250 g of ice at 0^oC is added to 600 g of water at 18.0^oC. What is the final temperature of the system?
questions.question_raw: In an insulated vessel, 250 g of ice at 0oC is added to 600 g of water at 18.0oC. What is the final temperature of the system?
questions.explanation:
maximum heat given by water is $Q = {m_{water}}s\Delta T $ =600 $\times$ 1 $\times$ (18 − 0) = 10800cal
Heat required to melt the complete ice = m_ice L = 250 $\times$ 80 = 20000cal
ice remains unmelt is given by = ${{20000 - 10800} \over {80}} = {{9200} \over {80}} $ = 115gm
Heat given by water is not sufficient to meet the complete ice. so that temp of the system is $0^\circ C$ with some ice remains unmelt

questions.question:
In an insulated vessel, 250 g of ice at 0$^\circ $C is added to 600 g of water at 18.0$^\circ $C. How much ice remains when the system reaches equilibrium?
given that latent heat of fusion of ice = 79.5 cal/gm, the specific heat of water = 1cal/gm^oC.
questions.question_raw:
In an insulated vessel, 250 g of ice at 0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C is added to 600 g of water at 18.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. How much ice remains when the system reaches equilibrium?
given that latent heat of fusion of ice = 79.5 cal/gm, the specific heat of water = 1cal/gmoC.
questions.explanation:
maximum heat given by water is
${Q_1} = {m_w}{s_w}\Delta T = 600 \times 1 \times (18 - 0) = 10800cal$
The heat required to melt the complete ice = Q_2 = m_ice L
Q_2 = 250 $\times$ 79.5 = 19875cal
Heat given by water is not sufficient to melt complete ice. Hence the temperature of the mixture will be $0^\circ C$ with some ice remains unmelt.
amount of ice melt = $m = {{{Q_1}} \over L} = {{10800} \over {79.5}} $ = 136gm
L = latent heat of fusion of ice = 79.5 cal/gm
Amount of ice which remains unmelt at equilibrium = 250 - 136 = 114 gm

questions.question:
If 90.0 g of molten lead at 327.3^oC is poured into a 300-g casting form made of iron and initially at 20.0^oC, what is the final temperature of the system? (Assume that no energy loss to the environment occurs.) Latent heat of fusion of lead = 2.45 $\times$ 10^4 J/kg, specific heat of lead = 128J/Kg^oC
specific heat of iron = 448J/Kg^oC.
questions.question_raw:
If 90.0 g of molten lead at 327.3oC is poured into a 300-g casting form made of iron and initially at 20.0oC, what is the final temperature of the system? (Assume that no energy loss to the environment occurs.) Latent heat of fusion of lead = 2.45  {tex}\times{/tex} 104 J/kg, specific heat of lead = 128J/KgoC
specific heat of iron = 448J/KgoC.
questions.explanation:
The melting point of lead is given by 327.3^o C.
Assume the final temperature of the system is T. Then the amount of energy released by the lead as it solidifies is given by 
ΔQ = m_leadL_lead = 0.09 kg*(2.45*10^4 J/kg) = 2205 J,
and the amount of energy released as it cools is given by :-
ΔQ = m_leadc_leadΔT = 0.09 kg*(128 J/(kg^oC))(327.3 ^oC - T) = (11.52 J/^oC)(327.3 ^oC - T).
This energy is absorbed by the iron. Thus,
2205 J + (11.52 J/^oC) (327.3^oC - T) = m_ironc_ironΔT = 0.3 kg*(448 J/(kg^oC))(T - 20 ^oC).
5975.5 J - (11.52 J/^oC)T = (134 J/^oC)T - 2688 J.
8663.5 J = (145.52 J/^oC)T.
T = 59.5^o C.

questions.question:
A surveyor uses a steel measuring tape that is exactly 50.000 m long at a temperature of 20$^\circ $C. The markings on the tape are calibrated for this temperature.
 What is the length of the tape when the temperature is 35^oC?
 When it is 35^oC, the surveyor uses the tape to measure a distance. The value that she reads off the tape is 35.794 m. What is the actual distance? $\alpha$ = 1.2 $\times$ 10^-5oC
questions.question_raw:
A surveyor uses a steel measuring tape that is exactly 50.000 m long at a temperature of 20{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. The markings on the tape are calibrated for this temperature.

  1. What is the length of the tape when the temperature is 35oC?
  2. When it is 35oC, the surveyor uses the tape to measure a distance. The value that she reads off the tape is 35.794 m. What is the actual distance? {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5oC
questions.explanation:
$\alpha = {{\Delta l} \over l}{1 \over {\Delta T}}$
$\Delta l = l\alpha \Delta T$ = 50 $\times$ 1.2 $\times$ 10^-5 $\times$ 15 = 0.009m
final length = 50 + 0.009m = 50.009m
if actual distance = x
then $\Delta x = x\alpha \Delta T$
x−35.794 = x $\times$ 1.2 $\times$ 10^−5 $\times$ 15
x = 35.800m

questions.question: A 200- ${\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ glass flask is filled to the brim with mercury at 20^oC. How much mercury overflows when the temperature of the system is raised to 100$^\circ $ C.The coefficient of linear expansion of the glass is 0.40 $\times$ 10^−5/^oC. Coefficient of volume expansion of mercury is 1.82 $\times$ 10^-4/^oC.
questions.question_raw: A 200- {tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{/tex} glass flask is filled to the brim with mercury at 20oC. How much mercury overflows when the temperature of the system is raised to 100{tex}^\circ {/tex} C.The coefficient of linear expansion of the glass is 0.40 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−5/oC. Coefficient of volume expansion of mercury is 1.82 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4/oC.
questions.explanation:
coefficient of linear expansion of the glass ${\gamma _S} $ = 0.40 $\times$ 10^−5/^oC
Coefficient of volume expansion of mercury ${a_L} $ = 1.82 $\times$ 10^-4/^oC
The number of mercury overflows
$\Delta V = ({\gamma _L} - {\gamma _S})V\Delta \theta$
${\gamma _S} $ = 3α = 3 $\times$ 0.4 $\times$ 10^−5 = 0.12 $\times$ 10^−4
ΔV = (1.82−0.12) $\times$ 10^−4 $\times$ 200 $\times$ 80 = 2.7cm^3
mercury overflows = 2.7cm^3
